engineering.translation: Krishna states the avatar doctrine: though unborn (aja) and imperishable (avyaya), though the Lord of beings, presiding over his own prakṛti, he comes into being through his own māyā. The verse holds two claims simultaneously — Krishna is metaphysically not bound by ordinary birth, yet phenomenally appears to take birth, by his own creative will. This is the largest metaphysical claim in chapter 4 and one of the largest in the Gītā. The engineering analog cannot honor the metaphysical claim. STRETCHED-tagged. The honest operational analog: the senior whose underlying expertise is invariant across stacks — what they actually know is independent of any specific framework — yet who appears in each new framework as if learning it freshly. The expertise is unchanging; the apparent re-learning is governed by the senior's choice to engage each new stack on its terms. The verse exceeds this; the analog respects the limit.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A principal engineer with a decade of database internals expertise joins a company that uses a database she has never personally operated — a newer Postgres-derived system with custom extensions. In the first week, she sits in an onboarding session and asks beginner questions about the extension's locking semantics. A junior engineer watching is confused: 'I thought she was supposed to be a database expert?' A peer says: 'She is. What you're watching is the senior choosing to engage the specific system on its specific terms. The expertise is in the underlying lock-manager principles, the storage-layer assumptions, the transaction-isolation models. Those are unchanging. The specific quirks of this extension — those she has to learn. She is choosing to take the form of a learner here, by her own deliberate decision, because that is the right way to engage a new system. Her expertise is unchanged. Her appearance has been adapted.' The junior nods. The metaphysical scope of the verse is larger than this; the analog operates at the operational layer only.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader takes it as a claim that engineers are avatars. The verse describes a metaphysical Being. The engineering analog respects only the structural pattern (invariant expertise + adapted appearance) and is explicitly STRETCHED. Anyone who reads this analog as engineers-as-divine has misread both the verse and the analog.
engineering.counter_example: When the senior's 'invariant expertise' is in fact stack-specific — when their database knowledge was actually Oracle-specific and does not transfer to Postgres — the analog's first half collapses. The diagnostic: when the senior engages the new stack, do they ask questions about underlying principles (locks, storage, isolation) or about surface syntax? Underlying-principle questions reveal invariant expertise; surface-syntax-only questions reveal stack-bound knowledge that cannot honor the analog.
engineering.implication: When you watch a senior ask 'beginner' questions in a new framework, watch what level the questions are at. Underlying-principle questions reveal invariant expertise being applied; surface questions reveal someone newly learning. Both are legitimate; only the first honors the structure the verse opens.
engineering.quotable_line: Invariant expertise + adapted appearance — the operational analog of the avatar's structure, far short of the verse's metaphysical scope.

iterations.mutation: v0: 4.6 is the avatar doctrine's first explicit statement (aja, avyayātmā, ātma-māyayā sambhavāmi). STRETCHED tagged honestly per chapter thesis. Engineering layer maps only to the structural pattern (invariant expertise + adapted appearance), not the metaphysical claim. Concrete scenario uses a principal database engineer joining a company with a custom Postgres extension, asking beginner questions on extension-specific quirks while her underlying lock-manager expertise is invariant. The analog explicitly disavows engineers-as-avatars.
